caveats: a: O*NET is occupation-level and includes shoe and leather production work outside NAICS 811430.
a: No industry wage-weighted task inventory was found; the range is intentionally wide.
rho: This is a bounded implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
rho: The frozen dataset reports no estimated firms in the LMM band, so implementation evidence from sufficiently scaled operators may be scarce.
e: The frozen n=0 estimate is not re-estimated here and makes the transferable-firm opportunity effectively unavailable in the dataset.
e: Repeat consumer use may be episodic rather than contractually recurring.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not this industry or the LMM band.
s5: Stated plans overstate completed qualifying transfers and do not distinguish every control structure.
q: No direct evidence was found on benefit sharing or repricing after automation.
q: Retention could differ materially between luxury restoration, commodity shoe repair, and institutional accounts.
d5: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes work outside the industry.
d5: EPA material is directional policy context, not an industry demand forecast.
d5: The range does not assume that circular-economy messaging converts into paid repair demand.
o: The proxy does not measure future robotics adoption.
o: Mail-in platforms may change which operator performs the work without eliminating operator requirement.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly consumer-paid, per-job repair charges, with some recurring commercial or institutional accounts and mail-in work; pricing is generally fixed by job after inspection rather than cost-plus.
lens.excluded_activities: Retailers primarily selling new footwear or leather goods, shoe shining without repair, repair of leather clothing classified elsewhere, captive repair units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US firms repairing footwear and leather or leather-like personal goods for external customers, including sole, heel, zipper, lining, handbag, belt, luggage, dyeing, cleaning, and related repair work, where customers use the service repeatedly or the firm has repeat referral and institutional demand.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the adjacent occupation Shoe and Leather Workers and Repairers rather than the industry's wage-weighted task mix. Its physical repair, inspection, cutting, stitching, gluing, polishing, and hand-tool tasks anchor a low substitution share, while estimating, payment, records, inventory, customer communication, and office-software work supply the exposed portion. Employer administrative labor would raise the share; owner-technician shops would lower it.
caveats: O*NET is occupation-level and includes shoe and leather production work outside NAICS 811430.
No industry wage-weighted task inventory was found; the range is intentionally wide.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-wage study of cobbler and leather-repair shops separating technician, owner-manager, customer-service, and administrative hours, paired with task-level AI trials for intake and estimates.
a.rationale: Most production work is dexterous, item-specific, and performed on physical goods. Generative AI can substitute portions of intake, quote drafting, customer communication, bookkeeping, marketing, scheduling, knowledge retrieval, and diagnosis support, but not the core bench work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for Shoe and Leather Workers and Repairers from 9,500 in 2024 to 9,100 in 2034, a 3.8% ten-year decline. Treating employment as a noisy proxy for constant-quality repair demand and translating the modest decline to a five-year horizon supports a base just below one. Productivity, imports, owner labor, replacement economics, and circular-economy interest can make employment fall faster or slower than service quantity.
caveats: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes work outside the industry.
EPA material is directional policy context, not an industry demand forecast.
The range does not assume that circular-economy messaging converts into paid repair demand.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year industry receipts and repair-unit volumes for NAICS 811430, split between footwear and other leather goods, plus customer survey data on repair-versus-replace decisions.
d5.rationale: The occupation projection suggests broadly stable to gently declining demand rather than collapse. Repair and reuse policy interest provides a tailwind, while cheap replacement goods and declining repair skills remain headwinds.

caveats: The frozen n=0 estimate is not re-estimated here and makes the transferable-firm opportunity effectively unavailable in the dataset.
Repeat consumer use may be episodic rather than contractually recurring.
e.change_evidence: A business-level census from industry directories and transaction databases that verifies revenue, EBITDA, independence, customer repeat rates, and control ownership for every US operator near the LMM band.
e.rationale: Repair is supplied to external customers and can generate repeat use, but many shops are owner-operated, local, and below the LMM threshold; the prompt's frozen n=0 is consistent with very limited availability at scale. The estimate is the eligible share conditional on an LMM-classified firm existing, not a revision to n.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 811430 ($73,119,920) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 811430 ($193,128,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $110,422 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 811430); class <5: 630 firms, avg revenue/firm $159,719 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,996 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $650,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $101,875 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,430,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $223,852 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,772,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $903,318 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 0

o.bridge: O*NET's physical repair-task inventory proxies for the service quantity that still needs an accountable operator. Stitching, cutting, gluing, clamping, polishing, fitting, inspection, and part replacement require custody of the item and skilled physical execution. Software may enable self-diagnosis or displace intake work, but it does not deliver those repairs; replacement rather than repair is captured mainly in d5.
caveats: The proxy does not measure future robotics adoption.
Mail-in platforms may change which operator performs the work without eliminating operator requirement.
o.change_evidence: Observed shares of repairs completed through customer self-service kits, automated kiosks or robotic systems, and software-only substitution across leading repair categories over five years.
o.rationale: Nearly all surviving repair quantity still needs a shop or craft operator to handle and alter the physical item. AI can support the operator but is unlikely to turn the service into pure software within five years.

caveats: No direct evidence was found on benefit sharing or repricing after automation.
Retention could differ materially between luxury restoration, commodity shoe repair, and institutional accounts.
q.change_evidence: Shop-level cohorts linking workflow savings to realized gross margin, repair prices, turnaround time, customer retention, and competitor repricing over multiple renewal or repeat-purchase cycles.
q.rationale: Per-job consumer pricing provides no automatic contractual pass-through, so an operator can retain some implemented labor benefit. Low switching costs, visible local prices, modest ticket sizes, and capacity-driven price competition should return part of the benefit to customers over five years.

caveats: This is a bounded implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
The frozen dataset reports no estimated firms in the LMM band, so implementation evidence from sufficiently scaled operators may be scarce.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month before-and-after operating data from footwear-repair chains or software vendors showing adoption, labor hours avoided, error rates, and sustained usage by workflow.
rho.rationale: Commodity office workflows can be implemented quickly with human review, but very small shops have limited systems, sparse structured data, and little change capacity. Diagnostic and quote assistance also require item photos, shop knowledge, and technician sign-off.

s5.bridge: Gallup measured stated five-year plans among US employer-business owners across industries: 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership. Applying the broad employer rate to eligible footwear-repair firms and discounting for plans not executed, transfers lacking a durable operation, and non-qualifying transactions produces the lower base. Older craft-business ownership could raise transfer pressure; the lack of scalable buyers could lower completion.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not this industry or the LMM band.
Stated plans overstate completed qualifying transfers and do not distinguish every control structure.
s5.change_evidence: Completed five-year ownership-transfer data for independent shoe and leather-repair firms from business brokers, state registrations, SBA lending records, or a verified owner survey.
s5.rationale: A broad employer-owner succession survey is informative but does not measure completed control transfers in this niche. The base discounts stated intention to an estimated qualifying completion probability.

principal_driver: Physical, item-specific bench work keeps almost all surviving service demand operator-required.
principal_weakness: The frozen estimate of zero LMM-band firms leaves little credible acquisition population despite a defensible service.
